display.title: Riot and Rebellion in Mexico: The Making of a Race War Paradigm
display.abstract: This study is not a history of riots and rebellions as such; rather, it describes the role that race played in the intellectual responses that government officials in Mexico elaborated when confronted with dissent from below. Ana Sabau made an interesting choice of topics to review: the massive colonial uprising of 1767 in the Bajío, the insurrection of Haiti and its impact on the imagination of New Spain's leadership, the Maya rebellion of 1847–1901, and the neo-Zapatistas of 1994 (briefly addressed in an epilogue). To undertake such an ambitious spatial and temporal project, Sabau closely analyzes a handful of documents generated by colonial and republican authorities as they sought to manage violent opposition. Her book “grapples with the specific workings of race war rhetoric in different contexts while also engaging with it as a paradigm” (p. 9).In the case of the Bajío riots in 1767, she dedicates a chapter to highlighting José de Gálvez's discussion of indios and castas. She insists that the famous visitador was concerned that free mulatos and indios vagos were “insufficiently racialized” because the region's dynamism had blurred caste and thus produced the need to “(re)generate racial difference in the Bajío” (p. 32). Gálvez's solutions to this—residential controls, tribute censuses, and labor discipline—reflected a “panoptical” colonial impulse in the service of capitalist economic development (p. 41). The following chapter uses the capitulaciones of the rebels to reveal that they seem much more concerned with taxes, the free movement of goods, and local livelihoods than racial resentments. In Sabau's view, however, resistance to taxes is an issue of race because the taxes were racialized “tribute.” In a “coda” to these two chapters, she combs through Hipólito Villarroel's description of Mexico City and argues that it is “a text in which the past and future of colonial racializing strategies can be parsed out” (p. 79). In a revealing comment, she recognizes that Gálvez's project had little long-term impact on the region (p. 52), and for that matter Villarroel's text went largely unread until it was published in 1831, indicating that the purpose of this volume is to describe the racial obsessions of Spanish officialdom and not to explore the social realities of the viceroyalty.The discussion of Haiti and its impact on the viceroyalty is the most insightful since it promises to place Mexico in a wider continental perspective. Sabau perceptively notes how the language around the Haitian Revolution changed between Juan López de Cancelada's 1806 publication of Louis Dubroca's La vida de J. J. Dessalines, in which López de Cancelada asserted in his introduction to the biography that Haitian society (especially in terms of race) was completely distinct from that of New Spain, and Bishop Manuel Abad y Queipo's panicky declarations four years later that the populations of Haiti and New Spain were almost alike (pp. 105–6). Additionally, she describes how viceregal authorities came to ban plays that incorporated themes concerning slavery, abolition, and interracial marriage, and she asserts that the censorship indicated how authorities “altered the racialization of New Spain's popular sectors; after 1808, Haiti's Blackness began to seem analogous to New Spain's Indianness” (p. 121). Unfortunately, there is no attempt to bring the events of 1810–21 into the discussion.The volume approaches the emblematic Caste War of Yucatán through two sets of documents. The first set explores the infamous deportation of Maya rebels as indentured workers to Cuba. Yucatecan authorities treated Maya individuals as if they were beyond the pale of citizenship, and Sabau adeptly relates this treatment to the widespread historical practice of penal labor in the Spanish empire. Sabau asserts that the counterinsurgency measure of controlling Maya through a passport system aimed to “spatially fix and police” racial distinctions (p. 154). The author jumps to unsubstantiated conclusions that the “penal forced migration” aspired to commandeer the lands vacated for the growing henequen economy given that the rebel territories were far from the region dedicated to the crop (p. 158). The second set of documents consists of an entertaining, if fanciful, appraisal of efforts to map the region in order to contain the rebellion. There is no doubt that effective maps assist counterinsurgency campaigns, and the vast blank spaces of the contemporary maps for the eastern third of the peninsula indicate how effectively the rebels impeded efforts to reconnoiter their space.Sabau places race at the center of historical processes, but she never defines what race was and how it changed over time. Subordinate people in this text do not determine their social personae; the power of the state imposes social identities on the “bodies” of its subjects. This leads us to the author's next assumption: that the state is powerful and effective. Gálvez “racializes” everything through tribute and creates an omniscient system to enforce it, while the money-grubbing ad hoc counterinsurgency schemes in Yucatán were part of the global expansion of capitalism. Although the author promises to explore “the rebel archive,” discussions of popular actors' perceptions of their own resistance are fleeting, and any idea of how they constructed their identities is absent. Finally, anachronism runs throughout the text: the race paradigm is timeless, equally applied to the Bajío in 1767 and Chiapas in 1994.

marc.value: Riot and Rebellion in Mexico: The Making of a Race War Paradigm
